verses.text: Job pleads only to be heard out; let them mock once he has finished. He asks the basic courtesy of listening. To answer a man before truly hearing him is folly and unkindness.

His complaint, he says, is not against men but God, and even he is appalled when he ponders it. The mystery frightens him. Some truths about God's dealings are so deep they make the godly tremble to consider them.

Job presses the hard fact: 'wherefore do the wicked live, become old, yea, are mighty in power?' Their homes are safe, their children settled. He overturns the friends' neat rule. The prosperity of the ungodly is a riddle no simple formula can solve.

The wicked send out their children like a flock, dance and sing, and 'spend their days in wealth,' going down to the grave with no lingering pain. Their lives seem all ease. Outward comfort is no proof of God's favour, nor its absence of His wrath.

Yet these very men say to God, 'depart from us... what is the Almighty, that we should serve him?' Their wealth, Job notes, is not truly in their own hands. Prosperity often breeds contempt for the God who gave it.

How often, Job asks, is the wicked's lamp really snuffed out in this life? The friends say God stores up judgment for his children, but Job answers, let the man himself feel it. Justice deferred to the next generation is small comfort to a watching world.

Shall anyone teach God knowledge, when one dies in full strength and ease, another in bitterness, and both lie down alike in the dust? Death levels all outwardly. The grave alone settles nothing; the real reckoning lies beyond it, with God.

Job knows their thoughts and their schemes to condemn him; let them ask any traveller what they have seen. He appeals to common observation. The honest eye sees a world more tangled than their tidy theology allows.

The wicked, Job says, is spared in calamity and honoured even at his funeral, so how can they comfort him with answers full of 'falsehood'? He exposes their empty consolation. Comfort built on a false view of God's ways only deepens the sufferer's wound.
